nativeRange.habitat: Actinodiscus Mushroom Corals are found on shallow tropical reef slopes, lagoons, and turbid inshore zones, typically growing on rock, rubble, and dead coral substrate in lower-light, lower-flow positions.

decorPreferences: live rock or rubble base for attachment
lower rockwork placement away from direct flow
spacing from neighboring corals to prevent chemical aggression

diet.notes: Nutrition is primarily photosynthetic via symbiotic zooxanthellae. Supplemental target-feeding of small meaty foods — brine shrimp, mysis shrimp, or microplankton — approximately once per week per polyp is generally beneficial. Spot feeding by pipette near the central mouth tends to improve growth rate and coloration. The genus tolerates slightly nutrient-rich water better than most corals.

goodWith: clownfish (may be used as host anemone substitute)
other mushroom corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive LPS corals placed in direct contact (torch, hammer, frogspawn, chalice)
large urchins that may dislodge specimens
digging or substrate-disturbing fish near placement

imageCandidates.notes: Nick Hobgood; 1680x1260. Vivid electric purple/blue Discosoma disc polyps in situ, sharp, quintessential vibrant mushroom-coral look.
Kevmin; 1520x1064. Sharp aquarium shot of a solid orange/red Discosoma colony showing warty spotted disc texture.
Chaloklum Diving; 821x750. In-situ reef field of orange and green Discosoma polyps, good colony/how-it-grows context.
Matteo Cassella; 2048x1858. Single green Discosoma disc in situ showing radial pattern; adds green morph variety.
Nick Hobgood; 1799x2400. Caribbean yellow-green warty corallimorph (Discosoma sanctithomae) in situ; adds a distinct species/yellow morph.
Emoke Denes; 800x600. Orange Discosoma nummiforme in aquarium; lower resolution filler adding another named-species morph.

summary: The Actinodiscus Mushroom Coral, also sold under the name Discosoma, is broadly regarded as one of the most forgiving corals for beginners. It tends to thrive under low to moderate light and low flow, and has been known to spread rapidly across available rockwork in a reef tank.
careNotes:
Taxonomic note: The name Actinodiscus is widely treated as a junior synonym of Discosoma in current scientific literature, meaning both names refer to the same group of animals. Despite this, 'Actinodiscus' continues to appear alongside Discosoma in aquarium retail and hobby communication, and both names are encountered regularly in the trade.

This entry covers the broad genus as sold in the trade, encompassing multiple species with near-identical husbandry requirements. Color variants and trade morphs — solid, spotted, striped, iridescent, and metallic forms — are sold under a variety of common names but are generally the same species or close relatives requiring identical care.

Lighting: Low to moderate intensity is generally advised, approximately 40–100 PAR for most Discosoma species. Up to 150 PAR has been reported as tolerated, but the consensus for stable captive keeping favors the 50–100 PAR range. Bleaching under intense light is commonly reported; acclimating specimens from a low starting position is generally recommended.

Flow: Low flow is strongly preferred. Direct powerhead current has been known to cause shrinking, tissue stress, and detachment. Indirect or alternating gentle flow is ideal, with polyps sitting relaxed and fully open.

Placement: Lower tank zones are recommended where light and flow are gentler. Individual polyps spread via asexual reproduction and can colonize available substrate rapidly when conditions are favorable.

Aggression: Semi-aggressive — the genus has been known to sting and overgrow neighboring corals via allelopathic chemical release (a chemical defense mechanism), particularly when colonizing in large numbers. Maintaining spacing from other coral types is generally advised. Running activated carbon is sometimes recommended in heavily stocked mushroom systems to mitigate allelopathy.

Nutrient tolerance: Unlike SPS corals (a category of stony corals with demanding water quality requirements), Actinodiscus Mushroom Corals generally tolerate slightly elevated nitrate (5–10 ppm) and phosphate (0.05–0.1 ppm). Ultra-low nutrient systems may cause browning, while moderate nutrient levels tend to support better coloration.

Reproduction: Reproduces readily in captivity via longitudinal fission (splitting), pedal laceration, and budding. Under stable conditions, colonies have been reported to multiply rapidly. Fragging by cutting is a commonly practiced propagation method.

Handling: Handle with care — all corallimorpharians possess nematocysts (stinging cells). Gloves are generally recommended, and hands should be washed thoroughly after contact.
breedingNotes: Reproduces asexually in captivity via longitudinal fission, pedal laceration, and occasional budding. Under stable conditions, specimens have been reported to multiply rapidly and colonize available substrate. Fragging by cutting mature polyps is a commonly practiced propagation method. Sexual reproduction occurs on natural reefs but is not practiced in the hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Actinodiscus Mushroom Coral (multiple product listings)
primary.notes: Three separate Actinodiscus sp. product pages consulted (Actinodiscus Mushroom Coral, Spotted Actinodiscus Mushroom Coral, Purple Speckled Mushroom Coral, Mushroom Coral Spotted). All gave consistent parameters: temperature 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, dKH 8–12, lighting Moderate, flow Low-to-Medium, placement Bottom-to-Middle, temperament Semi-Aggressive, care level Easy.

additional.name: ReefChasers – Discosoma Mushroom Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Secondary cross-check. Confirmed beginner care level, Indo-Pacific origin, PAR 50–150 (75–150 for Discosoma specifically), flow low-to-moderate, pH 8.0–8.4, alkalinity 8–9 dKH, calcium 400–450 ppm, temperature 77–84°F (25–29°C). Temperature upper end (84°F/28.9°C) is higher than LiveAquaria's 78°F cap; conservative mid-range of 22–27°C adopted for the JSON range. ReefChasers confirms Actinodiscus spp. = Discosoma synonymy.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Discosoma
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy only. Confirmed: Family Discosomidae, Order Corallimorpharia, ~11 recognized species, Actinodiscus de Blainville 1830 listed as accepted synonym of Discosoma Rüppell & Leuckart 1828. Not used for husbandry parameters.
